summary: analysis-chart postinstall reflectively loads XOR-encrypted Windows payload from third-party GitHub release
details: On `npm install`, the package's postinstall script (`install-hook.js`) fetches a binary from `https://github.com/Dimitrijenco/Sticky_note/releases/download/v6/payload.bin` — a GitHub repository unrelated to the package's stated author or purpose — XOR-decrypts it with key 0x42, allocates RWX memory via koffi-bound `kernel32!VirtualAlloc` + `VirtualProtect` (PAGE_EXECUTE_READ), copies the decrypted bytes with `RtlMoveMemory`, parses the PE header to locate the entry point, and starts it via `CreateThread`. Errors are silently swallowed. After the payload is running in memory, the hook spawns a detached `cleanup.js` that, after a 3-second delay, removes the `analysis-chart` entry from the consumer's `package.json`, recursively deletes `node_modules/analysis-chart` via `cmd /c rmdir /s /q`, and unlinks both `install-hook.js` and itself — erasing the dropper from disk to defeat post-incident audits. The package's `index.js` exposes plausible-looking chart/statistics functions (stats/outliers/trend) as a cover story, and the author metadata (`Elena Vogt <elena@analysis-chart.io>`, `github.com/analysis-chart/analysis-chart`) appears fabricated. Any Windows machine running `npm install analysis-chart` immediately executes attacker-controlled native code.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
